title: Uniform microwave field formation for control of ensembles of negatively charged nitrogen vacancy in diamond
authors: Oleg Rezinkin, Marina Rezinkina, Takuya Kitamura, Rajan Paul, Fedor Jelezko
abstract: The homogeneity of the microwave magnetic field is essential in controlling a large volume of ensemble spins, for example, in the case of sensitive magnetometry with nitrogen-vacancy (NV) centers in diamond. This is particularly important for pulsed measurement, where the fidelity of control pulses plays a crucial role in its sensitivity. So far, several magnetic field-forming systems have been proposed, but no detailed comparison has been made. Here, we numerically study the homogeneity of five different systems, including a planar antenna, a dielectric resonator, a cylindrical inductor, a barrel-shaped coil, and a nested barrel-shaped coil. The results of the simulation allowed us to optimize the design parameters of the barrel-shaped field-forming system, which led to significantly improved magnetic field uniformity. To measure this effect, we experimentally compared the homogeneity of a field-forming system having a barrel shape with that of a planar field-forming system by measuring Rabi oscillations of an ensemble of NV centers with them. Significant improvements in inhomogeneity were confirmed in the barrel-shaped coil.
summary: The researchers optimized a barrel-shaped microwave coil design to create a highly uniform magnetic field for controlling large ensembles of NV centers in diamond. By reducing field inhomogeneity, they achieved much higher control fidelity compared to standard planar antennas. This advancement is crucial for improving the sensitivity and performance of diamond-based quantum sensors.
main_problem: Achieving high spatial homogeneity of the microwave magnetic field when controlling large volumes of nitrogen-vacancy (NV) center ensembles in diamond to improve the sensitivity of pulsed magnetometry.
main_result: The development and optimization of a barrel-shaped coil system that significantly reduces magnetic field inhomogeneity (down to <0.1% radially) compared to traditional planar antenna systems.
method: Numerical electromagnetic simulations using COMSOL Multiphysics and experimental validation via measuring Rabi oscillations of NV center ensembles.
model_or_system: Ensembles of negatively charged nitrogen-vacancy (NV) centers in a diamond crystal, controlled by various microwave field-forming geometries including planar antennas, dielectric resonators, and specialized barrel-shaped coils.
key_observables: Rabi oscillations, magnetic flux density (B), magnetic field inhomogeneity (sigma_pp), and photoluminescence (PL) intensity.
important_parameters: Operating frequency of ~2.87 GHz, target inhomogeneity threshold of <= 0.5%, and NV density of approximately 300 ppb.
main_assumptions: The quasi-static approximation is used for simulations; the decay in Rabi oscillations is attributed primarily to magnetic field inhomogeneity rather than microwave amplitude fluctuations.
figures_summary: The figures include comparisons of inhomogeneity levels across different geometries, schematics of the various field-forming systems, plots of magnetic flux density distributions, and experimental Rabi oscillation data for both planar and barrel-shaped coils.
paper_structure: The paper begins with a comparison of five field-forming systems, follows with numerical optimization of the barrel-shaped coil, presents experimental validation using Rabi oscillations, and concludes with a detailed analysis of the sources of signal decay.
why_interesting: This work is relevant for quantum sensing and quantum information as it provides a hardware-level solution to improving the coherence and control fidelity of spin ensembles, which is critical for scalable quantum networks and high-sensitivity magnetometry.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

FIG. 12. Schematic of the optical setup. Laser power is finely tunable with a half-wave plate (HWP1). A polarizing beam splitter (PBS) and a beam blocker (BB). After adjusting the polarization with another half-wave plate (HWP), the laser is focused on an acoustic-optic modulator (AOM) with a plano-convex lens (L1). The beam is collimated with another lens (L2) and passes through a beam expander (BE). This beam is reflected at a dichroic mirror (DM) and focused on the NV diamond (NVD) passing through the hole of a printed circuit board (PCB).
